title: Payment Services Bill introduced in Parliament, proposing a licensing regime for digital payment token services
summary: Singapore's Payment Services Bill (Bill No. 48/2018) was read for the first time in Parliament on 19 November 2018. As introduced, the Bill proposes an activity-based licensing framework administered by the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) covering payment services, including a 'digital payment token service' category -- defined in the Bill to cover dealing in, or facilitating the exchange of, digital payment tokens such as cryptocurrencies -- alongside money-changing, domestic and cross-border money transfer, account issuance, merchant acquisition, and e-money issuance services. The Bill would repeal the Money-changing and Remittance Businesses Act and the Payment Systems (Oversight) Act, and make consequential amendments to several other Acts.
why_it_matters: As introduced, the Bill would require businesses dealing in or facilitating the exchange of digital payment tokens -- a category covering cryptocurrencies and similar tokens -- to hold a licence from MAS under the same activity-based framework used for other payment services, rather than leaving that activity outside MAS's payment-services licensing regime.

citations.quote: Read the first time on 19 November 2018
